metadata.research_scope: Washington ice and high-grade alpine routes

routes.difficulty_description: 45-50 degree ice slopes, ~800 ft of technical climbing
watch_out: Crevasse danger in high sections: endless bottom crevasse in snowfield near top
Serac exposure on upper terrain above 11,000 ft
Two significant ice pitches (35-40 and 45-50 degrees) requiring ice tools and protection
Fixed rappel line may or may not be present at drainage gully - verify current conditions
Snow bridge hazards documented in lower snowfield
Route-finding precision critical at lower elevations to locate snow/ice ramp

routes.difficulty_description: Long alpine snow route with early season conditions
watch_out: Significant rockfall and avalanche hazard in couloir - 'move quickly' emphasized
Early sun exposure warms frozen rock and triggers rockfall - early alpine start essential
Icefall exposure descending via Kautz Glacier: pass underneath looming Kautz Ice cliff and move very quickly
Large crevasses on upper Wilson Glacier requiring lengthy end-runs in late season
Bergschrund crossing potential routefinding complications at base of couloir
Soft afternoon snow conditions create hazardous descent - alpine start critical
Long day car-to-car ascent increases exposure window
Helmets highly recommended
Scout Wilson Glacier crossing from high camp evening before

routes.grade: AI2-3 with Grade IV overall, 40-60 degree ice climbing
routes.difficulty_description: Sustained steep ice and snow with serious alpine commitment
watch_out: Objective hazards from Thumb Rock itself and rock bands above at 10,500 ft bivy area
Active avalanche discharge documented: 'Avalanche from Willis Wall' photo evidence of active snow sloughs from adjacent terrain
Vertical ice pitches at bergschrund crossing (5-8 ft sections)
70-80 degree ice pitches near rock bands around 10,500 ft - crux terrain
Sustained 40-60 degree ice climbing approaching Liberty Cap Glacier
50-60 degree steep snow/ice faces on east ridge above Black Pyramid
Seasonal bridges and icy ledges require careful crevasse navigation
Intricate Carbon Glacier crossing - complex crevasse systems throughout
Extreme wind exposure at altitude: documented 80mph summit winds
Grade IV route - serious alpine undertaking requiring solid ice climbing and glacier travel skills
sources: Mountain Project route description and photos

routes.difficulty_description: Steep snow/ice climbing via cirque and narrow couloirs
watch_out: Substantial icefall exposure from Kautz Ice Cliff when accessing headwall
Must move rapidly through lower access chutes at 10,000 ft - minimize time under icefall hazard during warm months
Headwall receives a lot of sun in summer - accelerates rockfall and ice instability
Alpine start strongly recommended to minimize heating exposure
Numerous hidden holes on Nisqually Glacier crossing - rope up for safe passage
Rock bands at 11,300 and 12,200 ft may require scrambling or protection
Route best attempted when snow covers most of face
Direct glacier approaches melt out early in summer - resort to alternate access routes
Bergschrund hazard at both initial glacier crossing and Wilson Glacier approaches in late season

routes.grade: 40-55 degree ice and snow, AI2-3 with fixed rock section
routes.difficulty_description: Steep alpine snow/ice with icefall exposure and technical rock
watch_out: Bergschrund crossing requires finding bridge just left of rock outcropping - end-run may be necessary if impassable
Icefall danger: climbing between rock buttress (right) and icefall (left) with angles 45-55 degrees
Hanging ice cliff nicknamed 'doom cliff' - serious objective hazard
One short vertical rock step with one or two fixed pins - may be missing or unreliable
Condition-dependent: may encounter almost entirely snow climb OR substantial climbing on verglassed rock and water ice
Requires ice screws, pins, and rock gear depending on conditions encountered
Sustained steep snow climbing 40-55 degrees on ~1000 ft summit day gain
High elevation remote position on northwest side
Limited bivy sites until reaching Liberty Cap Glacier
Steeper and more sustained than adjacent Liberty Ridge - demands experienced fit climbers
sources: Condition variation documentation in trip reports

routes.grade: AI2-3 with Grade IV variation, 50-60 degree steep snow and ice
routes.difficulty_description: North face ice and mixed terrain above Mowich Lake
watch_out: Steep ice sections (AI2-3 grade) requiring technical protection
Very steep snow pitches 50-60 degrees on Grade IV variation
Mixed rock and ice terrain at 12,500 ft elevation
Grade IV line features sustained steep climbing with significant exposure
'Hour glass' snow, ice and rock terrain suggests terrain traps and instability
Limited protection placement opportunities on extended snow sections: only 'couple of screws for ice steeps and 2-4 pickets'
Two route variations (Grade III cuts left, Grade IV cuts right) - complexity adds navigation hazard
No explicit avalanche advisories provided - use judgment in snow stability assessment
Must arrange descent via Emmons or Disappointment Cleaver - navigation complexity

routes.difficulty_description: Glacier travel across convex slopes to upper Coleman and Deming glaciers
watch_out: Serac avalanches do occur here with some regularity - particularly in Black Buttes area below Colfax Peak
Multiple crevasse systems requiring proper rescue equipment
Major crevasse formations documented: teams navigating large crevasses at 8,800 ft
Active icefall hazards on route - climbers must navigate around
Convex snow slopes increase crevasse hazard - crevasses are hidden
Must bring snow pickets and crevasse rescue gear for safe passage
Roman Wall section requires careful navigation around upper Coleman and Deming glaciers
Grade II designation reflects moderate technical difficulty but substantial objective hazards
sources: Mountain Project route description and trip reports

routes.difficulty_description: 800 feet of steep ice climbing on northwest face
watch_out: Upper section: 800 feet of 60-degree ice (AI4, WI3) with little reliable protection
Ice slab instability: upper section ice slabs formed giant plate (1 inch thick) covering wet snow underneath
Two bergschrund crossings requiring careful navigation
Evidence of avalanche activity along route
Steep snow sections reaching 50 degrees in lower portions
Thin ice layers prone to instability and difficult climbing
Alpine terrain on northwest face involves significant weather exposure
Limited anchor options on main climbing sections - belays below rock bands use ice screws, upper pitches use pickets punched through ice layer
Seasonal variation in snow and ice stability - early May differs from later ascents
Monitor cascadeclimbers.com for current conditions
sources: Early May trip report with ice condition details

routes.difficulty_description: Long remote glacier route on Baker's northeast flank
watch_out: Crevasses requiring navigation through crevasse fields
Headwall section requiring careful route-finding
Long, remote route on Baker's northeast flank increases commitment and rescue difficulty
Glacier gear essential - carries full objective hazard of glacier travel
sources: Mountain Project route description (limited hazard detail available)

routes.difficulty_description: Exposed chimney system with ice, snow, and rock scrambling
watch_out: Steep and exposed terrain throughout - particularly dangerous during descent
Exposed down climbing through chimney sections requiring rappel stations
Active rockfall zones in off-route gullies - warning about 'moss or lichen covered rock' indicates these trap hazards
Large crevasses on Upper Curtis Glacier requiring standard glacier travel skills
Moat hazard: 'Watch out for the moat' near initial snow crossing
Upper Curtis Glacier starts to break up late season - deteriorating conditions create hazards
Late season when Winnie's Slide becomes iced: may need ice screws and second tool
Route-finding extremely difficult - 'People frequently report difficult route finding. Pay attention and don't overthink it!'
Multiple thin paths lead to cliff overlooks - serious hazard for disoriented parties
Significant weather exposure on dramatic Upper Curtis Glacier crossing
2-3 day commitment increases exposure risk
Self-registration required at Glacier ranger station for multi-day attempts
sources: Mountain Project route description and user warnings

routes.difficulty_description: Technical alpine rock with mixed terrain
watch_out: Loose rock and sometimes crumbly black lichen on rock at times
Alternative bypass gully NOT recommended - involves climbing dirty rock prone to rockfall
Gendarme section almost always in shade - can be very cold or even icy, creating hypothermia risk and icy climbing conditions
Significant complex terrain: 'parties have accidentally taken wrong descent path' - disorientation hazard
Seasonal constraints: snow patches affect water availability at notch (8,200 ft)
Multiple approach/descent options involve glacier travel (Sherpa, Stuart glaciers) that become 'too difficult to navigate' as season progresses
Route quality varies - some sections clean, others lichen-covered and unstable
20 pitches of mostly 5th class climbing with mixed terrain
Helmet essential due to rockfall concern
Complex terrain and poor visibility increase navigation challenges especially during descent
sources: Mountain Project route description and hazard warnings

routes.difficulty_description: Mixed ice and snow couloir on ice
watch_out: Water ice pitches up to 60 degrees - hardest ice conditions
Most of route at 40-50 degree angles (unstable snow/ice)
Crux: approximately 100 feet of water ice up to 60 degrees requiring ice climbing technique
Mixed terrain transitions between ice and rock - protection quality variable
Alpine ice climbing requiring strong technical skills

routes.difficulty_description: Classic North American alpine climb with mixed terrain
watch_out: Loose rock and lichen-covered sections throughout
Complex terrain with multiple approach/descent route options
Glacier travel hazards (Sherpa, Stuart glaciers) particularly late season
Water availability limited at notch (8,200 ft) depending on snowmelt
Navigation complexity - route-finding can be challenging
Weather exposure on open alpine terrain
Significant elevation gain over long day commitment

routes.difficulty_description: Alpine ice climbing on glacier terrain
watch_out: Alpine ice terrain with technical climbing
Crevasse hazards inherent to glacier climbing
Bergschrund crossing required
sources: Mountain Project Mount Stuart area page

routes.difficulty_description: Complex alpine ice and snow couloir climbing
watch_out: NOT recommended for beginners - 'really requires great deal of soloing or run-out climbing'
Sustained steep snow and ice pitches ('the runnels') separating first and second couloirs
Pitches take ice screws depending on conditions OR pitons and rock gear - variable protection
Significant unprotected climbing sections - serious consequences for mistakes
Requires comfort on steep snow and WI3 climbing ability
Photos document 'whiteout on descent' and 'bad weather' - rapid condition deterioration likely
High alpine location (Dragontail Peak in Enchantments, >9,000 ft) - weather can turn quickly
Couloir format inherently presents rockfall danger
Sparse natural protection on mixed terrain: small nuts, 4-5 pins, cams (.5-2) and 4-5 ice screws needed
Advanced winter mountaineering abilities required
sources: Mountain Project route description and photos

routes.difficulty_description: Alpine snow couloir with variable steepness
watch_out: Constant 40-50 degree angle with slightly steeper sections half way up depending on year
Middle section can melt out early in season - creates instability and avalanche risk
Rock band near summit requires navigation through exposed stone
Objective hazard from falling rock during warmer conditions when snow melts around exposed stone
Seasonal guidance suggests 'Late May and early June typically have best conditions' - poor conditions outside this window
Endless bushwhacking through slide alder on approach - adds fatigue and cumulative exposure time hazard
Grade III alpine terrain demands winter mountaineering experience and conservative decision-making
Protection needed: pickets if uncomfortable with steep snow/easy ice, set of stoppers, webbing for raps

routes.difficulty_description: Steep ice climbing with variable conditions
watch_out: Route is condition-dependent: 'When fully in, Razorblades offers 3 fantastic pitches of ice'
May not be climbable during all conditions - early season may have poor ice formation
Steep funky climbing on first pitch - suggests unstable or unpredictable ice conditions
Crux pitch (P1): WI5+, 120 feet - steep technical climbing with high difficulty
Second pitch: short steep curtain (WI4) - bulges and difficult placements possible
Third pitch: long straight forward WI5 - endurance and protection placement challenge
Alternative option: can walk around pitch 1 on left if not solidly formed - early season workaround
Water flow hazard: large chunk of greenish ice pouring down cliffs indicates active water flow feeding ice formation
Active water affects both conditions and ice stability
Ice screws required for protection

routes.difficulty_description: Mega classic with multiple ice pitches and avalanche terrain
watch_out: Approach crosses avalanche terrain - documented D1 avalanche debris observed ('enough to kill you')
Drainage itself marked as avalanche-prone - high risk of wet slab activity
River crossing hazard: frozen river must be crossed on approach
Deceptive conditions: 'If river isn't frozen at slow points, flow is probably also deceivingly not frozen' - may be impossible or dangerous during warm periods
Tentative ice quality on initial pitches - highly variable conditions
Must search for 'most frozen line' rather than following established passages
Thick ice sections unreliable for anchors - protection placement challenges
Climbing difficulty ranges WI3-WI4+ depending on ice formation and line choice
P1: WI2+/WI3 finding most frozen line up main flow
P3-5: varying WI3-WI4+ with difficult start to crux flow
Mixed terrain with technical moves on steep snow traverses
Semi-hanging belays on upper pitches with limited protection
Weather-dependent formation: may appear frozen from road but be PG-13 slush conditions when approached
Climbers frequently find deceptive conditions despite solid appearance from distance
sources: Mountain Project route description and condition warnings

routes.difficulty_description: Classic Icicle Creek ice formation
watch_out: Icicle Creek subject to seasonal raptor closure: 1/2 mile radius of Bridge Creek Wall closed January 1 through August 15 for golden eagle nesting
Conditions dependent on winter freeze-thaw cycle - not reliable every season
High water hazard during thaw periods
Approach hazards typical of creek canyon access
sources: Mountain Project Icicle Creek ice area page

routes.difficulty_description: Hubba Hubba area ice climbing
watch_out: Subject to same seasonal raptor closure as other Icicle Creek routes
Seasonal ice formation unreliability
Creek canyon approach hazards
sources: Mountain Project Icicle Creek ice area page

routes.difficulty_description: Mixed ice and climbing on buttress
watch_out: Eightmile Buttress location subject to seasonal conditions
Mixed climbing requiring both ice tools and rock gear
Raptor closure applicable to Bridge Creek Wall area
sources: Mountain Project Icicle Creek ice area page

routes.difficulty_description: Committing alpine couloir and ridge route
watch_out: Extremely committing - very long approach with lots of up and down: difficult to retreat once committed
Total mileage 40+ miles (up Frostbite, down Cool Glacier) - multi-day commitment increases exposure
Most parties take 2-3 days - extended alpine exposure risk
Seasonal variation: 'Route may be snow in early season, expect more ice later in year'
Snow slopes up to approximately 45 degrees with steep angle exposure
Class 3 rock scrambling sections on ascent
Alpine ice face requiring 1-2 pitches of technical climbing
'Rabbit ears' notch landmark can be disorienting - navigation hazard
Careful navigation required to avoid steeper snow/ice climbing beyond rabbit ears
Must know descent route and consider pre-programming GPS coordinates
Infrastructure issues: 'best approach may vary year to year' due to occasional washout of bridges/roads
North Fork Sauk Trail may be best starting/ending point - long approach logistics
Equipment: rope, pickets, ice screws with small to medium nuts optional

routes.difficulty_description: Glacier approach to Glacier Peak summit
watch_out: Glaciated terrain requiring technical glacier travel skills
Ice axe and crampons essential for crevasse hazard mitigation and steep snow
High elevation (10,541 ft) with significant altitude exposure
Navigate corridor left of feature dividing glacier's lower section
Snow chute near summit - avalanche potential on steeper terrain
9-mile approach with significant elevation gain to base
High alpine location indicates substantial exposure to mountain weather
Extended glacier crossing creates commitment hazard
Route lacks explicit warnings about specific hazards like avalanche danger or crevasse fields
sources: Mountain Project route description (limited hazard detail)

routes.difficulty_description: Ridge and glacier route on Glacier Peak
watch_out: Alpine ice and mixed terrain exposure
Glacier travel hazards on Cool Glacier
Navigation complexity through multiple basins

routes.difficulty_description: Glacier climbing on Washington's second highest peak
watch_out: Alpine ice terrain at high elevation (12,276 ft)
Steep snow slopes requiring careful glacier travel
Multiple glacier systems on mountain create navigation complexity
Weather exposure at summit altitude
sources: Mountain Project Mount Adams area page

routes.difficulty_description: Snow ridge approach to summit
watch_out: Moderate snow climbing at high elevation
Ridge traverse creates exposure hazard
Water availability at notch dependent on seasonal snowmelt
sources: Mountain Project Mount Adams area page

routes.difficulty_description: Ice and steep snow on north face
watch_out: Alpine ice climbing requiring ice tools and crampons
Steep snow sections with exposure
PG13 rating indicates moderate protection and hazard concerns
sources: Mountain Project Mount Adams area page

routes.difficulty_description: Alpine mixed climbing on remote peak
watch_out: Remote mountain - long approach and difficult rescue access
Mixed ice and rock climbing requiring versatile skills
Alpine terrain with weather exposure
Mount Index described by Fred Beckey as 'one of most difficult summits to attain in the Cascades'
North Peak approach hazards
Loose rock typical of Cascade peaks
sources: Mountain Project Mount Index area page

routes.difficulty_description: Scrambling with steep rock sections
watch_out: Chossy steep section in upper portion - loose or unstable rock
Summit block has 'steep and roofy overhead' terrain - exposure on direct finish
Solid red rock in lower sections transitions to smooth high-featured slab mid-section
Most climbers do this unroped despite class 3-4 terrain - exposure is real
Route-finding tricky, going too far west toward Red Mountain Pond indicates navigation challenges
Summit section decision point: easier scrambling (west side) or harder, exposed climbing (direct finish)
Descent sections could use rappelling equipment

routes.difficulty_description: Glacier and rock approach via Cascade Pass
watch_out: Roping up on glaciers generally good idea - Sahale Glacier small and not very active
3rd and 4th class scrambling after glacier - exposed terrain
Short chimney-like feature to summit - route-finding required
Final pitch rated 5.0 (5th class rock) - falling rock potential
Grade II alpine classification indicates moderate technical difficulty
8,500 ft elevation - high alpine weather exposure
Avalanche danger at Cascade Pass during high-risk periods
Route lacks specific warnings about crevasse fields, weather exposure, or avalanche risk assessment

routes.difficulty_description: Alternative approach via glacier and rock
watch_out: PG13 rating indicates moderate hazard concerns and moderate protection
Glacier approach with exposure on final sections
4th class rock scrambling with falling rock potential

routes.difficulty_description: Legendary traverse across North Cascades peaks
watch_out: 2-3 day alpine commitment with multiple peaks crossed
Mixed terrain with rock, ice, and snow
Extensive glacier travel across multiple systems
Weather exposure on high altitude ridge traverse
Navigation complexity across multiple peaks and saddles
Spider Glacier crossings and other technical sections

routes.difficulty_description: Granite alpine rock climbing
watch_out: Snow and ice present early in season through June
Best climbing season: late June through September
Exposed alpine terrain with weather hazard
Rappel descents on multi-pitch routes
Rock quality varies - loose sections possible
Northwest Forest Pass or America The Beautiful Pass required for trailhead access
Early season climbing requires knowledge of current conditions
sources: Mountain Project Washington Pass area page

routes.difficulty_description: Direct glacier headwall ascent avoiding crevasses
watch_out: 45-degree headwall of Ingraham Glacier - steep snow/ice section
Avoids many small and some huge crevasses - route planning critical
Upper portion has low angle switchbacks - less steep but route-finding required
Summit rim attainment at 14,100 ft - altitude exposure
20-30 minute final walk from crater rim to Columbia Crest
Summer conditions bring hidden crevasse hazards and moats

routes.grade: Hourglass Gully winter variation: 4th class, 1 pitch
routes.difficulty_description: Winter alpine variation on Mount Index
watch_out: Winter route requiring ice climbing or snow climbing skills
Short ice pitch potential in winter conditions
Alpine terrain exposure in winter conditions
Remote location increases rescue difficulty
sources: Mountain Project Mount Index area page
